concept.title: Known limitations
concept.shortdesc: Understanding the current limitations of markdown-validator before you write rules saves hours of debugging. Four limitations affect the types of rules you can reliably express today.

othermeta.name: description
othermeta.content: Know the current limitations of markdown-validator before writing rules: flat HTML DOM, absence of a negation operator, multi-element equality behavior, and NLTK dependency.

section.title: Limitation 1: Flat HTML — within-section queries are not reliable
section.p: markdown-validator renders Markdown to HTML using the standard markdown library. This library produces flat HTML: all headings are direct children of <body>. There are no <section> wrappers that group a heading with its content.

This means XPath queries that rely on containment — "find a link that is inside the Prerequisites section" — will not work as intended. The query .//h2[1]/following-sibling::a does not return links within the first H2's section; it returns every link anywhere after the first H2 in document order.

What to do instead: Write rules that check element presence and order using absolute position (for example, .//h2[1] for the first H2), not containment. For sections, count the heading and rely on positional predicates.
Planned fix: A structured-DOM parser mode is planned for v0.4.

section.title: Limitation 2: No negation operator
section.p: There is no negate field or not operator. Expressing "the title must NOT contain the word 'guide'" requires a workflow-inversion workaround that is difficult to read and error-prone. See the absence-check pattern in Rule design patterns.
Planned fix: A negate: true field on RuleModel is planned for v0.3.

section.title: Limitation 3: Multi-element equality checks are usually wrong
section.p: When an XPath query returns more than one element and you use "operation": "==", the evaluator requires every element in the result set to equal the expected value. This is almost never the intended behavior.

Example of the problem: A rule targeting ".//h2[last()]/preceding-sibling::h2" (to find the second-to-last H2) returns all H2s before the last one. Requiring all of them to equal "Clean up resources" fails on any document with more than two H2s.
What to do instead: Target the specific element you want by position, not by relative relationship. Use ".//h2[last()-1]" to get the second-to-last H2 directly.

section.title: Limitation 4: NLTK language data must be installed separately
section.p: Rules that use the s (sentence count) or p<N> (part of speech) operators require NLTK language corpora that are not included with the pip package. A fresh environment will fail with a LookupError on first use of these rules.
What to do: Include the NLTK download step in your team's onboarding instructions and CI pipeline. See Download NLTK language data.
